FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=10,828 youth from ABCD Release 4.0, baseline and one-year follow-up)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time variables: self-reported screen time on weekdays and weekend days, dichotomized as >2 versus ≤2 hours/day per behavioral guidelines; separately assessed withdrawal/depressed, anxious/depressed, and somatic complaint outcomes

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports multilevel logistic regressions testing associations between baseline sleep duration/screen time and follow-up internalizing symptoms; bidirectional models test internalizing symptoms predicting behaviors; reported with odds ratios and p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Extensive numeric reporting: Table 3 reports adjusted ORs with 95% CIs (e.g., OR 1.6 [95% CI 1.1–2.2, p=.0129] for weekend screen time→withdrawn/depressed in females; OR 0.6 [95% CI 0.4–0.8, p=.0007] for sleep duration)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using longitudinal design; cross-lagged analyses examining both baseline behaviors→outcomes and baseline symptoms→behaviors over one year

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper reports cross-sectional analyses (sleep/screen time as both IV and DV at different timepoints), and screen time is used as both IV predicting outcomes and DV predicted by internalizing symptoms; thus directionality is mixed and not exclusively longitud-only with demographic predictors. Also reports sleep/SM as IVs predicting non-SM psychological outcomes (internalizing symptoms).
notes: Female-specific associations between weekend screen time and withdrawn/depressed symptoms (OR 1.6) observed; unidirectional relationships (behaviors→symptoms, not vice versa). E8 applied: inferential testing (logistic regression with p-values and CIs) present throughout.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (n=4,641 ages 9-12 at baseline, Year 1, and Year 2 follow-up; discovery sample n=2,359; replication sample n=2,282)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen media activity (SMA) as hours/day spent on non-school-related activities (TV, videos, gaming, texting, video chatting, social networking); SMA measured at baseline, Year 1, Year 2

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) testing bidirectional associations between sleep duration/difficulties, SMA, and psychopathology symptoms; mediation analysis examining thalamus-PFC-brainstem structural covariation; multiple statistical tests reported with coefficients and p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Extensive numeric reporting: β coefficients with 95% CIs (e.g., β=-0.10 [95% CI: −0.16, −0.03], p=0.004 for SMA→shorter sleep; β=0.07 [95% CI: 0.01, 0.13], p=0.014 for externalizing→SMA); indirect effect for mediation reported with CI and p-value

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data using longitudinal cross-lagged panel models and mediation analysis; reports discovery and replication results; not a measurement-only paper

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2024; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper includes individual psychological/clinical predictors (externalizing/internalizing symptoms, DIMS) as key variables; reports SMA as both IV and DV in different models; includes mediation analysis with psychological mediators; reports cross-lagged relationships not exclusively longitudinal-demographic.
notes: E3 applied: latent structural covariation (thalamus-PFC-brainstem) pathway extractable with specific β coefficient. E8 applied: inferential testing (CLPM with β, CIs, p-values) throughout. Bidirectional associations found: higher SMA predicts shorter sleep (β=−0.10), and externalizing symptoms predict future SMA (β=0.07); externalizing partially mediates brain structure→SMA relationship.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=10,544 youth, baseline 2017-2018 and one-year follow-up 2018-2019; mean baseline age 9.9 years, 48.9% female, 45.4% non-White)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time variables by type: streaming (TV/movies), gaming, socializing (social networking); reported daily minutes on weekdays and weekend days; separate extractable coefficients for each screen type

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports isotemporal substitution linear regression models and compositional data analysis (CoDA) testing associations between baseline screen time types and follow-up BMI z-score; multiple models with reported coefficients and CIs

FT_IC4.evidence: Extensive numeric reporting: β coefficients with 95% CIs for screen time substitutions (e.g., replacing 30 min socializing with physical activity: β [95% CI]=−0.03 [−0.05, −0.002] in females; replacing socializing with sleep: β=−0.03 [−0.05, −0.01] in males)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD data using isotemporal substitution and CoDA methods; reports sex-stratified models examining screen time types predicting BMI; not a measurement or protocol paper

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity, 2024; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Screen time is used as the INDEPENDENT variable predicting a non-SM outcome (BMI); BMI is the dependent variable. EC7 only applies when screen time/SM is exclusively the dependent variable with demographic-only predictors in longitudinal analyses. Here, screen time predicts BMI, meeting the criterion for inclusion.
notes: E2 applied: isotemporal substitution and CoDA allow screen-time-specific coefficients to be extracted separately for streaming, gaming, socializing. E8 applied: inferential testing (linear regression with β, CIs) throughout. Sex-specific associations observed: in females, replacing socializing/streaming/gaming with physical activity associated with lower BMI; in males, replacing socializing/streaming/gaming with sleep associated with lower BMI.
